snp2.9:1.1:
This sutta mentions the metaphorical notion of “heartwood” (sāra) three times.
Although sāra as a metaphor is often translated as “essence,” this misses some of the metaphor’s implications.
When x is said to have y as its heartwood, that means that the proper development of x yields y,
and that y is the most valuable part of x—
just as a tree, as it matures, develops heartwood, and the heartwood is the most valuable part of the tree.
“With what virtue, what behavior,
snp2.9:1.3: would a person become rightly based
snp2.9:2.1: “One should be respectful of one’s superiors & not envious;
snp2.9:2.2: should have a sense of the time for seeing teachers;
snp2.9:2.3: should value the opportunity when a talk on Dhamma’s in progress;
snp2.9:2.4: should listen intently to well-spoken words;
snp2.9:3.1: should go at the proper time,
snp2.9:3.2: humbly, casting off arrogance, to one’s teacher’s presence;
snp2.9:3.4: the Dhamma, its meaning, restraint, & the holy life.
snp2.9:4.2: established in Dhamma, with a sense of how to investigate Dhamma,
snp2.9:4.3: one should not speak in ways destructive of Dhamma,
snp2.9:4.4: should guide oneself with true, well-spoken words.
snp2.9:5.4: one should go about free of intoxication, with steadfast mind.
snp2.9:6.1: Understanding’s the heartwood of well-spoken words;
snp2.9:6.2: concentration, the heartwood of learning & understanding.
snp2.9:6.3: When a person is hasty & heedless
snp2.9:7.1: While those who delight in the Dhamma taught by the noble ones,
snp2.9:7.2: are unsurpassed in word, action, & mind.
snp2.9:7.3: They, established in calm, composure, & concentration,
snp2.9:7.4: have reached what discernment & learning have as their heartwood.”
